main_country.name: Asteria
main_country.short_history: Asteria emerged from the fragmentation of the Orelian Empire in 1722. The First Union (1722-1801) established a feudal constitutional monarchy. The Industrial Awakening (1820-1880) brought rapid urban growth but deepened regional disparities. A failed republican coup in 1856 led to the Second Compact, which centralized power but preserved the monarchy as a figurehead. The Great Border War (1912-1916) redrew the eastern frontier. Post-war reforms slowly expanded suffrage and weakened hereditary guild privileges.
main_country.government_type: Constitutional monarchy with a bicameral parliament (Hereditary Council and Elected Chamber) and a Grand Chamberlain as head of government.

geography: Asteria occupies the central portion of the continent of Thanor. The Spine of Aethon, a mountain range running north-south, divides the country: to the west, the fertile Windward Plains and the Bay of Whispers; to the east, the arid Eastern Reaches and the Serpent River basin. The northern region is the Thornwood, a cold taiga with scattered mining settlements. The southern coast is a jagged archipelago known as the Shattered Isles, with mild winters and stormy summers. The central highlands contain Lake Silvershield, the largest freshwater lake in Thanor.

regions.name: Windward Plains
regions.character: Agricultural heartland, dominated by grain estates and horse-breeding. Strong local nobility, historically rebellious.

regions.name: Eastern Reaches
regions.character: Semi-arid plateau with sparse population, known for sheep herding and mineral extraction. Frequent border disputes with neighboring Cerethia.

regions.name: Thornwood
regions.character: Northern taiga, heavily forested. Logging and iron mining. Sparse settlement, strong egalitarian traditions among lumber and mining guilds.

regions.name: Bay of Whispers
regions.character: Western coastal region, major trading ports (Havenport, Marinersgate). Cosmopolitan, with a mix of languages and old merchant families.

regions.name: Shattered Isles
regions.character: Archipelago of volcanic islands. Fishing, shipbuilding, and stubborn local autonomy. Known for a distinct dialect and isolationist leanings.

languages.name: Astrian
languages.notes: Official language, derived from Old Orelian. Used in government and education. Has northern and southern dialects that are barely mutually intelligible.

languages.name: Thornic
languages.notes: Spoken in the Thornwood and parts of the Eastern Reaches. No official status, but taught in local schools. Uses a different script in informal contexts.

languages.name: Archipelec
languages.notes: Creole of Astrian and old island tongues, spoken in the Shattered Isles. Contains many nautical loanwords. Stigmatized by mainlanders.

historical_periods.name: Orelian Dominion
historical_periods.summary: Asteria was a periphery province of the Orelian Empire, ruled by appointed governors. Rising taxation sparked local revolts.

historical_periods.name: First Union
historical_periods.summary: Federation of five duchies under a weak monarch. Frequent civil wars over succession. Ended with the Treaty of Silvershield.

historical_periods.name: Industrial Awakening
historical_periods.summary: Rapid urbanization and canal-building. Guilds became powerful economic players. Social unrest led to the 1856 coup attempt.

historical_periods.name: Second Compact
historical_periods.summary: Centralized state under the Crown. Expansion of bureaucracy and standing army. Tensions with Cerethia culminated in war.

historical_periods.name: Post-War Restructuring
historical_periods.summary: Redrawing of borders, slow democratization. Creation of the Elected Chamber. Continuing struggle between industrialists and traditional landowners.

historical_periods.name: Modernization Era
historical_periods.summary: Economic boom and cultural liberalization. Sputtering decline of heavy industry. Rise of regionalist movements.

political_structure: Asteria is a constitutional monarchy. The monarch (the Grand Duke/Duchess) holds ceremonial powers. Real executive authority lies with the Grand Chamberlain, appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Elected Chamber. The legislature is bicameral: the Hereditary Council (seats held by noble families and guild heads) and the Elected Chamber (popularly elected every five years by limited male suffrage, expanded to universal in 1967). The judiciary is nominally independent but historically influenced by the Crown. Local governance is divided into 5 regions with appointed governors (revoked in 1923, reinstated in 1972, creating tensions).
economic_structure: Mixed economy: agriculture (wheat, barley, wine) in the Windward Plains; mining (iron, copper, rare earths) in the Eastern Reaches and Thornwood; manufacturing concentrated in Havenport and Silvershield City; a declining textile sector in the north; a small but growing service and tech sector in the south. Guilds still control many trades through licensing. Banking centered on Havenport. Currency: the Astrian Crown (pegged to gold, but devalued in the 1930s). Smuggling is a major issue along the eastern border and through the Shattered Isles.
cultural_traits: Strong tradition of guild-based apprenticeship and festivals (e.g. the Midsummer Forging, the Week of Guilds). Food: hearty stews, flatbread, fish from the coasts. Literature: epic poems about the First Union, recent revival of Orelian-era folktales. Music: fiddles and drums in the north, reed instruments in the south. Religion: worship of the Æther (a nebulous divine force) with local saints/heroes. Clan loyalty is important in the Thornwood and Isles. Social hierarchy is less rigid than a century ago, but old nobility still commands deference.

naming_conventions.people: Given name + patronymic (male: -son, female: -dotter) or clan name for northerners. Southerners often use a family name derived from place. Examples: Alaric Thorwaldsen, Kaelina Marinsdotter, Erlan of Silvershield. Nobility use a territorial title like 'Lord of Havenport'.
naming_conventions.places: Suffixes: -port (coastal town), -burg (fortified town), -feld (plain), -mere (lake), -hold (castle), -reach (region). Prefixes: Siliver-, Thorn-, Wind-, East-. Examples: Silvershield, Havenport, Thornburg, Windfield.
naming_conventions.institutions: Often named after their founder or location, plus a descriptor: Silvershield University, the Royal Guild of Miners, the Thornwood Lumber Commission. Acronyms are uncommon except for modern agencies.
naming_conventions.sources: Norse-derived given names, Scottish/Germanic place-name patterns, English-style institution names.

historical_tensions: Central authority vs. regional autonomy: the Windward nobles resent taxation by Havenport bureaucrats.
Guild privileges vs. free trade: urban guilds block competition, causing friction with rural entrepreneurs.
Religious division: the Æther orthodoxy from the south conflicts with the more animist traditions of the Thornwood and Isles.
Border dispute with Cerethia: the Demeter Valley, claimed by both states since 1916, remains a militarized zone.
major_institutions: The Hereditary Council (upper house of parliament)
The Royal Guild of Miners (regulates all mining in the Eastern Reaches)
The Thornwood Lumber Commission (semi-public corporation, notorious for corruption)
The Astrian National Bank (central bank, created 1851)
The Cerethian Front Institute (think tank on border policy)
The Order of the Silver Key (secretive society of archivists and historians)
irregularities: Oldhaven (city) was renamed Newhaven in 1823 after a flood, then officially reverted to Oldhaven in 1931 amid local protests.
The 1856 Republican Coup failed because the rebel leader's horse stumbled on the parliament steps – the event is mockingly called 'Stumble Day'.
The Land Reform Act of 1892 was repealed after three years due to violent resistance by nobles; only 12% of land was redistributed.
The eastern border with Cerethia is marked by a river that changed course in 1904, leaving pockets of disputed territory.
The Thornwood region uses a different calendar (based on local saints) for religious festivals, causing bureaucratic confusion.
The national anthem has three verses, but the third verse is officially banned because it glorifies the failed republican coup.
real_world_avoidance_rules: No reference to any real country, city, river, mountain, or other geographic entity.
No real historical figures, events, or movements (e.g., no French Revolution, no World Wars, no Cold War).
No real languages, alphabets, or naming systems from Earth. All names must be fabricated.
No real institutions, companies, or organizations. Use fictional ones.
No real religions or belief systems. Fictional only.
Ensure all dates and years are within 1700-1995 and do not align with real-world events in a recognizable way.
Avoid any fictional world that mirrors a real region too closely (e.g., no 'Fantasy Britain' or 'Fantasy Japan').
Do not use any words or concepts that are trademarked or strongly associated with existing fiction (e.g., 'Mordor', 'Narnia').
